cleaned_sections.strain_interpretation: Strain This indicator measures how you feel about your environment. It relates to how safe, satisfied and connected you feel to your community and neighbourhood. It also measures whether you experience your home and neighbourhood to be conducive to the quality of your life. The extent to which you live your life indoors will impact on your strain score in this area. This is a subjective measure and does not indicate that you are safe or unsafe; it merely taps into whether you feel safe or unsafe. It therefore does not provide an objective measure of the quality of your environment. Ben your perceived level of Environmental strain is LOW. ● You feel connected and quite positive about your environment and community despite your collective challenges ● You feel safe in your neighbourhood and home ● You are satisfied with how much time you spend outdoors or in your broader community ● Experience your home environment as comfortable and conducive to getting things done ● You are not distracted from your core focus by environmental and service delivery issues in your community Vocational Strain This indicator measures how you are feeling about your career choices and work. It relates to how passionate you are about your work and how enjoyable it is to you at present. Your score will also be impacted by the amount of stress you experience at work and the quality of your relationships at work. If you are unemployed or have made alternative vocational choices at present, this will also impact on your strain score in this area. This is a subjective measure and does not indicate performance at work. It merely taps into your perceptions about your current vocational reality. cleaned_sections.decision_framework: The capacity to make effective, well-considered decisions is a critical competency in a professional environment. Effective decisions involve three phases or types of activity, namely: EXPERIENCE: Actual experience, data scanning and data collection ESAHP INTELLIGIBILITY: Interpretation and making sense of experience or data COMMITMENT: Applying judgement, making the decision and taking actions Your decision style is not only strongly impacted by your dominant Center of Intelligence but also by your main Ennea type and the amount of strain you are experiencing at present. Effective and wise decisions are made when all three Centers of Intelligence are positively engaged in the decision making process. Decisions happen in context. The more you are able to consider and position the context within which you need to make decisions, the more effective your decisions become. Action Centered Decisions Ben your dominant Center of Expression is Action Centered, and is likely to impact on your decision making in the following ways: ● Quick and immediate interpretation of information ● Able to make effective decisions under time pressure and in crisis situations ● Preference for acting to planning ● Willingness to swi�ly adapt decisions to changing circumstances ● Willingness to take risks Your Action Center is likely to lead you to emphasise the Commitment Phase of decision making as you are likely to move through the first two phases quickly, make gut decisions and want to act on these immediately as far as possible The drawbacks of this approach to decision making include: ● Reactive and impulsive decisions ● Not sufficiently aware of and considerate of impact of decisions on others ● Quick decision making style limits collaboration and input from others ● Hasty, impatient and high risk decisions can have problematic long term consequences ● Instinctive responses may be difficult to explain and justify to others cleaned_sections.strategic_leadership: Strategic leadership is the capacity to relate, align and maximise the internal direction of the organisation to the external world and the macro-environmental factors impacting on the organisation and its success. Ben, as an Ennea 8, your strategic leadership is likely to be influenced by your core drivers and motivations in the following ways: Visioning At Ennea 8 you have the potential to access the capacity for visionary leadership, but run the risk of becoming attached to outcome and having things your way. You are naturally more inclined towards the big picture but may tend to develop the vision intuitively, assuming that others know what you are thinking. You may rely quite heavily on your “gut” when developing the vision and deciding on the direction of the company or team. You may not always share the vision with others explicitly. This may be because you expect others to know the vision or believe that it is not necessary for others to know the vision as long as they understand what is expected of them. You are also likely to emphasise action towards the vision once it has been set. When developing the vision, you may at times be tempted to do so alone. This being said, you are likely to also know how to leverage the inputs and gain the support of key stakeholders and influencers in the team and business. Your challenge is to learn how to truly collaborate at the point of vision development, rather than collaborating with the end product already clearly in mind. Strategic Thinking You should enjoy the art of strategy. When dealing with strategic issues, your interest and effort is likely to directly relate to the challenge and potential impact of the matter at hand. Big challenges with a high potential level of impact are likely to be of much greater interest to you than low-impact that are not challenging. This may impact on the consistency with which you apply yourself. You may be quite ambitious and dedicated in your efforts to steer the company or team towards success. In doing so, you should enjoy strategic involvement in different aspects of the business but may become quite impatient with the details of implementation and execution of the strategy. You are likely to have high expectations of people in this regard, expecting them to comply with decisions and make things happen. Even if you are quite adept at dealing with the detail, you may be very impatient when doing so. Once you have made up your mind on an issue, you may be quite slow to change this, being very attached to outcome. This may lead you to miss cues from the external environment and can exacerbate strategic dri�. At the same time, once a decision has been made, you are likely to want and expect immediate action and results and may be quite demanding and impatient in the process. As a result others may experience you as somewhat intense, inflexible and pushy. cleaned_sections.team_dynamics: Within a team environment, your Enneagram type is likely to influence not only how you interact with others, but also what you view as ideal team relationships and behaviours. Teams go through different phases of development. Tuckman’s four stages of team development, namely Forming-Storming-Norming-Performing, illustrate the process through which teams go as it becomes more effective over time. Each of these phases are necessary if a team wants to grow towards high performance. Ideally teams move through the first three phases rapidly, spending most time in the Performing phase. In reality, some teams get stuck and struggle to move beyond the Forming and Storming phases. Your Impact on Team Ben, to understand the impact you have on your team, you will need to consider the phase of team development your team is currently in. You may find that different teams in different areas of your life are in different stages, which will impact on the way you show up in each group. The different Ennea types also differ in terms of: ● Types of goals that resonate with you ● Level of interdependence that you are most comfortable with ● Typical role played in the team As an Ennea 8, you prefer goals that are: ● Clearly aligned to strategic priorities and the big picture ● Set by you rather than enforced by others ● Aligned with what you see as critical given the task at hand ● Not restrictive and allow for opportunities to exert influence and make things happen ● Designed to move the team or organisation forward Interdependence and Team Role Ben, you are likely to be quite aware of interdependencies in a team setting that leave you at the mercy of others competence in task completion. As such, you prefer team environments where each person’s role is clear and territory is defined, leaving you with room to move and make things happen. When working with trustworthy individuals who have proved their competence to you, the team experience is likely to be much more enjoyable than when the opposite is the case. You therefore enjoy some independence but may also enjoy higher levels of interdependence with the proviso that the rest of team are competent. From a task perspective, your role in the team is likely to emphasise issues of leadership and strategy. You may therefore find yourself trying to focus the team on the bigger picture instead of getting stuck in tactical and operational matters. At the same time, you are likely to emphasise the role and importance of leadership in ensuring team effectiveness. From a relationship perspective, your role may be to challenge others and “name the elephant in the room” nobody wants to talk about. As such you may find yourself creating momentum and focus in the team through both driving and opposing moves. As long as people are reliable and hold up their end of the agreement, you will be comfortable letting them run with things. In this process, you are likely to still be very clear about your expected outcomes and deliverables. Your delegation style is likely to be firm, straight talking and potentially overwhelming as you expect people to say 'yes' and simply comply with your instructions. If people have not yet proven themselves to you, you may prefer to remain firmly in control. Performance Management Your big presence may mean that people enter into performance discussions with you being somewhat anxious or even fearful. Your feedback style is likely to be no-nonsense and forthright. If people have not met performance standards, they will probably already have been told this by you before the formal performance meeting. You are likely to respond well to a similarly direct response from others and may become quite aggressive or dismissive with people who don't take accountability or face the music. People may experience you as being hard to please. Those who perform well are likely to enjoy your generosity and backing. Motivation As there is inherent motivation for you in making things happen, you may believe that others have to take responsibility for their own motivational levels. You may need to become more sensitive to the fact that what motivates and drives you, may seem overwhelming and frightening to others. People within your circle of trust will however know your protection and support, which may inspire them to take courageous action and emulate your drive. Your demanding nature may however also end up crushing people's motivational levels. You may however also be tempted to simply forge ahead, believing that the organisation or team will eventually catch up. When tackling change projects, your impatience may drive you to expedite plans and push for immediate implementation. In doing so you may run the risk of running with changes at a pace that others struggle to keep up with. It may also lead you to overlook some of the people aspects involved in the change process, especially around issues of loss and closure. You may be quite attuned to resistance and adept at unravelling and pushing through resistance, but when doing so there may be casualties along the way. Your approach to resistance may be quite confrontational. You may also know how to leverage relationships to gain commitment from key role-players. Your ability to create urgency may however at times create urgency driven by anxiety rather than urgency driven by a positive image of the future. This may hamper change efforts. development_exercise_context.centers: : The Center of Intelligence Here are some ideas on practices and approaches that support the expression of all The Centers in an intelligent, present and mindful way. As your Action Center is most likely to be over-expressed, you can support a more balanced and healthy expression of this Center through the following exercises: ● Build self-awareness regarding the distinction between action energy that is grounded and alive vs. action energy that is unfocused and impulsive. ● Tune into your body as a regular practice by noticing and paying attention to the sensations of the body you are experiencing in the moment. You can use deliberate relaxation and breath-based exercises to do so. ● Focus on becoming more awake and attuned to The Centers of expression that are not as dominant, namely Feeling and Thinking. This entails slowing down the pace of processing, decision making and action to allow for reflection and analysis. ● Request feedback from others regarding the impact you are having on processes. For example, check in during a meeting with your colleagues on how they are experiencing your contribution and act on that immediately. ● Practice conscious movement such as yoga, tai chi or aikido to recalibrate the connection you have with your body and with pace. ● Pay attention to the sensations you are experiencing while doing everyday tasks such as chewing food, washing your hands or simply breathing in and out.
